title: My Experience as a Gene Research Volunteer
article_text: I originally wanted to be a mathematician, but I chose to study genes instead. I worked as a volunteer in a lab that was studying severe genetic diseases. The prospect of helping people gave me comfort. Firstly, I had to learn how to calculate the sequence of DNA. Secondly, I needed to integrate data from massive experiments. One criterion for success was retaining a clear record of every plot of data. I used to think the work was plain and boring, but I was wrong. The lab was installing new equipment, and I helped to set it up aside from my usual tasks. Some people offered criticism of our methods, but we took it as a bet to improve. My neck often hurt from looking at a screen, but I never complained because I loved the work.

image_prompts: A young person sitting at a desk with a book titled 'Mathematics', looking thoughtfully at a DNA model.
A volunteer in a white lab coat working with microscopes and test tubes in a bright laboratory.
A person smiling while looking at a glowing image of a healthy family, feeling comfort.
A student writing numbers and a DNA sequence on a blackboard with a calculator nearby.
A researcher combining two large piles of data charts on a computer screen.
A scientist pointing at a graph on paper, with a checklist labeled 'Success Criteria'.
A person frowning at a simple boring chart, then later smiling at an interesting one.
A lab with new equipment being installed, with a worker helping aside from regular tasks.
Two scientists talking, one pointing at a criticism note, the other holding a betting slip.
A person rubbing their neck after long hours at a computer, but still smiling at the work.
